title: Forest and Wildlife Resources - Analytical and Competency Based Test

questions.text: Humans and living organisms form a complex ecological web. Logically, why are forests considered the foundational element of this system?
options: They are the largest habitats on Earth.
They are primary producers on which all other living beings depend.
They absorb all the carbon dioxide produced by human activities.
They house apex predators which control all other populations.

questions.text: The implementation of the Indian Wildlife (Protection) Act in 1972 indicated a shift in environmental policy. What was the primary thrust of this programme regarding endangered species?
options: Relocating all endangered species to secure biological parks.
Banning hunting, providing legal protection to habitats, and restricting wildlife trade.
Encouraging local communities to domesticate wild animals.
Focusing purely on the protection of plant species rather than animals.

questions.text: The central government initiated projects to protect gravely threatened species. Logically, why would three different types of crocodiles (freshwater, saltwater, and Gharial) be explicitly specified in these protections?
options: To highlight that biodiversity conservation focuses on specific genetic and ecological diversities rather than generalized groups.
Because they are the only reptiles found in Indian rivers.
To simplify the legal jargon in the Wildlife Protection Act.
Because only saltwater crocodiles were facing threats from the trade.

questions.text: By comparing the tiger population at the turn of the century (55,000) to the 1973 figures (1,827), what can be analytically deduced about the environmental landscape prior to Project Tiger?
options: The decline was a natural evolutionary cycle uninfluenced by human activity.
Unregulated threats like poaching, habitat shrinkage, and prey depletion pushed the species to the verge of extinction.
The prey base species expanded too rapidly, creating an ecological imbalance.
Traditional medicines had completely replaced the need for tiger products.

questions.text: Project Tiger is often viewed not just as an effort to save an endangered species. What is its broader ecological objective?
options: To maximize international funding for Indian forests.
To serve as a means of preserving biotypes of sizeable magnitude.
To control the overpopulation of herbivores in reserve forests.
To transition tribal populations out of forested areas.

questions.text: A researcher is visiting a Tiger Reserve located in Uttarakhand. Which of the following reserves are they visiting?
options: Sariska Wildlife Sanctuary
Bandhavgarh National Park
Corbett National Park
Manas Tiger Reserve

questions.text: The notifications under the Wildlife Act in 1980 and 1986 marked a significant evolution in conservation planning. What was the nature of this evolution?
options: The focus shifted entirely from fauna to flora.
Insects like butterflies, moths, beetles, and a dragonfly were added to protected lists, showing a focus on broader biodiversity rather than just mega-fauna.
Marine life was exclusively prioritized over terrestrial animals.
Conservation planning was handed over entirely to private organizations.

questions.text: If a state government wishes to maximize the area where conservation of both forest and wildlife is strictly prioritized over any other usage, which category of forest should they primarily expand?
options: Protected Forests
Reserved Forests
Unclassed Forests
Community Forests
questions.topic: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

questions.text: A forest patch is legally designated as a 'permanent forest estate'. Based on the classifications provided, what can be logically deduced about this forest's management purpose?
options: It is maintained strictly for wildlife breeding without any human entry.
It is maintained for producing timber and other forest produce, and for protective reasons.
It belongs to private individuals and is unregulated by the government.
It is designated solely for ecological farming by local villagers.
questions.topic: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

questions.text: Analyzing the forest distribution data, why do states like Gujarat and the North-eastern states have a very high percentage of 'unclassed forests'?
options: Because these regions lack valuable timber species.
Because a large portion of forests in these areas is managed by local communities rather than exclusively by the government.
Because they have not yet passed any wildlife protection laws.
Because the central government has abandoned conservation efforts there.
questions.topic: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

questions.text: Which state's forest management strategy has resulted in the largest area under permanent forests, constituting 75 percent of its total forest area?
options: Madhya Pradesh
Jammu and Kashmir
Andhra Pradesh
Kerala
questions.topic: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

questions.text: The declaration of Bhairodev Dakav 'Sonchuri' by the inhabitants of five villages in Alwar is a prime example of what conservation dynamic?
options: Villagers relying entirely on government regulations to stop hunting.
Local communities establishing their own rules to protect wildlife against outside encroachments, explicitly rejecting government involvement.
The failure of local institutions to manage large tracts of land.
Corporate partnerships in local afforestation programs.

questions.text: In assessing the impact of citizens' groups like the Beej Bachao Andolan and Navdanya, what analytical conclusion can be drawn about modern agriculture?
options: Synthetic chemicals are absolutely necessary for economically viable crop production.
Adequate levels of diversified crop production without synthetic chemicals are both possible and economically viable.
Ecological farming results in unviable yields that cannot support local communities.
Traditional crop varieties are obsolete and should be entirely replaced.

questions.text: How does the Joint Forest Management (JFM) programme create a symbiotic relationship between local communities and the forest department?
options: By transferring absolute ownership of the land to the villagers in exchange for taxes.
By allowing local institutions to undertake protection activities on degraded forest land in return for intermediary benefits like non-timber forest produces.
By employing villagers as government officials to patrol pristine national parks.
By allowing commercial timber logging companies to operate alongside village councils.

questions.text: The concept of 'Sacred Groves' relies on what foundational premise for its success in preserving virgin forests?
options: Strict penalties imposed by the Indian judicial system.
Age-old tribal beliefs that all creations of nature have to be protected, leading to a complete ban on interference by local people.
Funding from international environmental organizations.
The high commercial value of the timber found within them.

questions.text: Assertion (A): Sacred qualities are often ascribed to plants and animals in Indian society. Reason (R): The Mundas and Santhal worship the tamarind and mango trees during weddings. Evaluate the statements based on the text.
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A (they worship mahua and kadamba).
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.

questions.text: The villagers in Sariska Tiger Reserve successfully combated mining activities. What was their primary legal instrument in this struggle?
options: The Joint Forest Management Resolution of 1988
The Forest Rights Act
The Wildlife Protection Act
The National Biodiversity Act

questions.text: According to the passage, conservation preserves the genetic diversity of plants and animals. Why is this genetic diversity specifically crucial for fisheries?
options: It prevents the introduction of invasive species into rivers.
Fisheries are heavily dependent on the maintenance of aquatic biodiversity for better growth and breeding of species.
It allows fisheries to transition to terrestrial farming methods.
It solely protects the Gharial population in the Girwa River.

questions.text: In 1991, plants were added to the list of protected species for the first time. How many plant species were initially protected?
options: Six species
Ten species
Several hundred species
One hundred species

questions.text: Which of the following states is NOT listed among those having a bulk of their forest area under 'protected forests'?
options: Bihar
Punjab
West Bengal
Odisha
questions.topic: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

questions.text: Assertion (A): The tiger population became highly vulnerable to illegal trading and poaching. Reason (R): India and Nepal provide habitat to about two-thirds of the surviving tiger population in the world. Which of the following is true?
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.

questions.text: What is the ultimate lesson derived from the dynamics of both environmental destruction and reconstruction in India?
options: Urbanization must be completely halted to save forests.
Local communities everywhere have to be involved in some kind of natural resource management.
Only central government laws can effectively preserve biodiversity.
Foreign investment is required to manage degraded forest lands.

questions.text: If a developmental activity is proposed in a forested region, what criteria must it meet to be acceptable according to the text's concluding thoughts?
options: It must be heavily industrialized and export-driven.
It must be exclusively managed by the forest department.
It must be people-centric, environment-friendly, and economically rewarding.
It must prioritize wildlife conservation over any human livelihood.

questions.text: The Chipko movement in the Himalayas is celebrated not just for resisting deforestation, but for proving what ecological concept?
options: That community afforestation with indigenous species can be enormously successful.
That commercial pine plantations yield the highest economic returns.
That joint forest management is ineffective in mountainous terrain.
That the government must own all unclassed forests.

questions.text: In and around Bishnoi villages in Rajasthan, what prevents the harm of local wildlife like herds of blackbuck and peacocks?
options: Heavy police presence and government fencing.
The animals' aggressive nature toward outsiders.
The animals are seen as an integral part of the community due to traditional conservation methods.
The lack of poachers in the desert region.

questions.text: State A has categorized 33% of its forests to protect them from further depletion. State B has categorized 51% of its forests as the most valuable for conservation. What types of forests do State A and State B primarily possess, respectively?
options: State A: Unclassed Forests; State B: Protected Forests
State A: Protected Forests; State B: Reserved Forests
State A: Reserved Forests; State B: Unclassed Forests
State A: Permanent Estates; State B: Protected Forests
questions.topic: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

questions.text: Which state is known for passing the first resolution for Joint Forest Management (JFM) and in what year?
options: Rajasthan in 1972
Madhya Pradesh in 1973
Odisha in 1988
Uttarakhand in 1991

questions.text: According to the excerpt provided regarding Gharials, their population has hit its lowest point since which decade?
Why is the reliance on traditional crop varieties cited as a reason for conserving forest and wildlife resources?
options: Because traditional crops are the only food source for the Indian elephant.
Because it highlights the importance of preserving the genetic diversity of plants for better growth and breeding.
Because it reduces the need for protected forest lands.
Because traditional crops grow faster in reserved forests.

questions.text: Based on the text, what distinguishes 'Unclassed Forests' from 'Reserved' and 'Protected' forests?
options: They are the only forests where hunting is legally permitted by the state.
They encompass other forests and wastelands belonging to both the government and private individuals or communities.
They are regarded as the most valuable for the conservation of wildlife.
They are completely devoid of any flora and fauna.
questions.topic: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources
